transcript.presenting_concern: Relapse prevention skills — managing triggers in social settings

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Welcome back everyone. Good to see all six faces here today. Let's start with our opening ritual — take a moment, feet on the floor, one deep breath together. [pause] Good. Now, as we check in, I want you each to rate your week on a scale of 1-10 in terms of recovery difficulty — 1 being easy sailing, 10 being white-knuckle the whole time. And then share what happened. Our focus last week was on identifying high-risk situations and practicing our refusal skills. Who'd like to start?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'll go. Difficulty rating: maybe a 5. So, my brother had a barbecue on Saturday. I knew there'd be drinking — there's always drinking. My first instinct was to make an excuse and skip it. But then I remembered what we talked about — avoidance versus coping — and how avoiding everything forever isn't sustainable. I need to learn to be around alcohol without using. So I went.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I appreciate you naming that deliberation. The decision to go was intentional, not impulsive. And how did it go?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My uncle offered me a beer within five minutes of walking in. Literally before I'd even set my keys down. "Hey Jason, grab a cold one from the cooler." And I used the broken record technique — "No thanks, I'm good." He kind of paused, pushed once more — "Come on, one won't kill you" — and I said "Really, I'm good, got an early morning tomorrow." I kept my voice casual, didn't make it a big deal. Then I went straight to the cooler and grabbed a Coke. Put it in a koozie so it looked like a beer. And that was that. Nobody said another word about it all afternoon.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Group, what do we notice about Jason's approach? What worked?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: He had a plan going in. He didn't just wing it and hope for the best. He'd already decided what he'd say if offered. And he gave himself an excuse that shut down further questioning — "early morning." People accept that.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: And he got a replacement drink in his hand quick. That's key — if your hand is already holding something, people stop offering. The koozie thing is smart too. Makes it invisible.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: He also didn't isolate once he was there. He didn't go hide in the corner feeling sorry for himself. He engaged.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: All great observations. Jason, how did it feel to be at the barbecue sober? Like, the actual experience of being there without alcohol?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Honestly? Kind of boring for the first hour. Like I was watching everyone else at a party through a glass window. They're all loosening up, getting louder, laughing at dumber stuff. And I'm just... here. Aware. But then around hour two, something shifted. I actually had real conversations with my cousin about his job. I played with my niece — she's four and hilarious. I drove home remembering everything. I woke up Sunday morning without a hangover for the first time after a family event in probably ten years. It was... different. Not bad. Just different.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That "different" feeling is your brain adjusting to socializing without the chemical shortcut. Alcohol provides artificial social lubrication — it quiets the inner critic, lowers inhibitions. Without it, you're more self-aware, which can feel uncomfortable at first. But it does get more natural over time as your brain creates new associations. Any cravings during the event?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: A flicker when I saw my uncle open his beer and heard that psst sound. Like a 3 out of 10. Gone in maybe thirty seconds. I noticed it, acknowledged it — "there's a craving" — and went back to my conversation.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's surfing the urge. Beautiful. You noticed it without acting on it, and it passed. That's the skill in action. Thank you, Jason. Rebecca, how was your week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Difficulty rating: probably a 7. I had a harder week. My ex-husband called Tuesday about the custody schedule and we got into it. He wants to swap weekends next month for his girlfriend's birthday trip and I said no because it's my weekend and I've already made plans with the kids. He got nasty. Said I was being vindictive. Said I only care about controlling things. I was shaking by the time we hung up.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What happened next?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My first thought — immediate, like muscle memory — was "I need a drink." Not want. Need. I was standing in my kitchen literally looking at the cabinet above the fridge where I used to keep the vodka. My hand went up toward the handle and then I remembered — it's empty. I cleared it out six weeks ago when I started this group. There was nothing to take.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the environmental control worked. What did the urge feel like physically?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like a pull. Like a magnet in my chest pointing toward the cabinet. My mouth was watering. My heart was racing. The urge was a ten. Full-body craving. I could almost taste the vodka — that burn in the back of my throat. It was scary how real it felt.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And what did you do?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I called my sponsor. Margaret. She picked up on the second ring. She didn't try to talk me out of it or lecture me — she just let me vent. I told her everything Mark said, how angry I was, how much I wanted to drink. She said "You're allowed to be angry. You're not allowed to drink about it." We stayed on the phone twenty minutes. By the end I was calmer and the urge was maybe a three. Then I took a hot bath and went to bed early.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Several important things here, group. Let's unpack this. First — the environmental control of not having alcohol in the house. That delay between craving and access is often the difference between relapse and recovery. If Rebecca had vodka in that cabinet, what would have happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] I would have poured it. Honestly. I know that about myself now. In that moment, I didn't have the willpower to resist if it was right there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that's not weakness — that's neuroscience. In a craving state, the prefrontal cortex — our decision-making center — goes offline. The limbic system takes over. Willpower is a prefrontal cortex function. So removing the substance from your environment is not cheating — it's strategic. Second important element: Rebecca recognized the emotional trigger immediately. Anger from the ex. She named it. Third — she called support. She didn't try to white-knuckle it alone. Group, what do we call this full sequence?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The chain analysis. Trigger — which was the phone call and the anger. Then the thought — "I need a drink." Then the urge — full-body craving. Then the choice point — and she chose coping over using.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. And at the choice point, Rebecca stacked multiple coping strategies: environmental barrier, social support, self-soothing with the bath, and sleep. That's the arsenal we've been building. Rebecca, one question — you said the craving was a ten. In past episodes before treatment, when the craving was a ten, what typically happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I drank. Every time. A ten meant I was drinking. Period.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So this week, for the first time, you experienced a ten-level craving and didn't drink. That's a milestone. I want you to recognize that.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [getting emotional] I didn't think of it that way. I was so focused on how close I came that I didn't think about the fact that I didn't. That I actually... didn't.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's huge, Rebecca. Really.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Seriously. A ten and you stayed sober? That's warrior-level stuff.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How does it feel to hear that from the group?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [wiping eyes] Good. It feels good. I've been beating myself up all week for almost relapsing and hearing you all say "you didn't" is... yeah. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The "almost" is a near-miss. We learn from near-misses in the same way pilots debrief near-crashes. What do you want to do differently to prepare for the next phone call from Mark?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've been thinking about that. I might need to limit calls to text or email only. When I hear his voice, my emotional brain activates. Text gives me time to respond instead of react. I'm going to ask him to keep communication in writing about custody.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's excellent boundary-setting. And it removes a significant trigger from your routine. Good. Marcus, your turn.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Difficulty rating: I'll say a 4 this week. No major triggers. I worked, went to the gym, went to meetings. But I want to connect to what Rebecca said because anger is my primary trigger too. For me it's my boss. Every time he gives me feedback — even constructive — I feel this rage. Like my skin is on fire. And then I want to use. Not drink — my thing is cocaine. But same pattern. Someone disrespects me, I feel powerless, and the drug makes me feel powerful again. Invincible. Like I could tell anyone off.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: There's the function of the substance — and it's important everyone hears this. Addiction is not just about feeling good or avoiding feeling bad. It serves a function. Rebecca uses alcohol to manage anger and emotional pain. Marcus uses cocaine to manage feelings of powerlessness. When we can name the function, we can find replacement behaviors that serve the same emotional need without the destruction. Marcus, what else makes you feel powerful?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The gym, honestly. When I'm deadlifting heavy, I feel like a beast. Nobody's talking down to me in that moment.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the gym serves the same function as cocaine — it gives you a sense of power and dominance — but without the crash, the money, the legal risk. Have you noticed whether gym days correlate with lower cravings?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Actually... yeah. I go Monday, Wednesday, Friday. Those are my easiest days. Tuesday and Thursday I'm more restless.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What if you added a short workout on Tuesday and Thursday? Even twenty minutes — pushups, pull-ups at home?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I could do that. I have a pull-up bar.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's try it this week. Track whether it changes your craving patterns on those days. Linda?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Difficulty rating: 6, but not for the reasons you'd think. My trigger isn't anger — it's loneliness. Friday night, kids at their dad's house for the weekend, empty house, nothing to do. That's my danger zone. Wine was my company. My friend. My ritual — pour a glass, watch TV, pour another, watch TV, pour another until the bottle was gone and I'd passed out on the couch.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What happened this Friday?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I felt the pull starting Thursday night actually. Just knowing Friday was coming and I'd be alone. By Friday at 5 PM when I got home from work, the house was so quiet I could hear the clock ticking. I sat on the couch for ten minutes doing nothing, just feeling... empty. And then I forced myself to go to my AA meeting at 7 even though every cell in my body was saying "stay home, stay comfortable."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And the meeting?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It helped. I talked. I shared about the loneliness. Someone came up after and said "I feel that too — Friday nights are the worst." We exchanged numbers. Her name is Jen. She's also divorced, also has her kids on alternating weekends. She invited me to coffee on Saturday morning.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Linda, that's excellent. You matched the function — loneliness trigger, connection solution. The meeting provided the social contact that wine was substituting for. And you're building new relationships with people who understand. How was Saturday coffee?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Really nice actually. We talked for like two hours. About the divorce, the kids, the drinking. It was like she was living a parallel life. I felt... seen. In a way I haven't in a long time.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That "seen" feeling — that's the antidote to loneliness. Connection is not just about being around people. It's about being known. That's what you found with Jen. I want to encourage you to keep building that. Now, Carlos — how was your week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Difficulty rating: probably a 9. And I need to be honest. I had a slip. Wednesday night.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you for naming that up front. Tell us what happened.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I was at a work dinner. Client entertainment. Everyone was ordering cocktails, wine with dinner. I'm the VP of sales — these dinners are part of my job. I told myself I'd have one glass of wine with dinner because it's a professional setting and I literally cannot be the only person at the table not drinking without fielding questions I don't want to answer in front of clients.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So one glass was the plan. What happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: One became two during the appetizer. Two became three with the main course. The waiter kept topping off. The clients were ordering bottles. By dessert I'd had three full glasses. Maybe three and a half. I stopped there — didn't continue after dinner, went straight home. But I felt... defeated.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How did you feel physically after? Were you impaired?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Not really. Three glasses over two hours with food — I wasn't drunk. I drove home fine. But it's not about that. It's that I said one and it became three. I lost control of the number. And I felt like a fraud — sitting in this room every week talking about sobriety and then drinking at dinner.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you for that honesty. It takes courage to come in here and say that. Group, what do we know about slips versus relapses?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: A slip is an event. A relapse is a pattern. One slip doesn't erase four months of work. A slip becomes a relapse when you either hide it, give up because of shame, or use it as permission to keep using. Carlos is here, telling us about it. That's not relapse behavior.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Well said. Carlos, the fact that you're here being honest — that IS getting back on track. Shame wants you to hide. Recovery asks you to be transparent. You chose transparency. Now let's examine the chain. What was the thought right before the first glass?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "I can't be the only one not drinking. They'll think it's weird. They'll ask questions. It'll become a thing. Maybe they'll think I have a problem and that'll affect the business relationship."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Social pressure thought combined with professional consequences fear. What was the emotion?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Anxiety, I think. Fear of standing out. Of being different.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the function for you — at least in that moment — was social conformity and anxiety management. Interesting, because your primary pattern is usually stress-related drinking. This was a different pathway. Now — what could you do differently next time? Group, help Carlos problem-solve.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The soda water with lime trick. I'm telling you — bartenders know this move. You order a club soda with lime, they put it in a rocks glass, it looks exactly like a vodka tonic or a gin and tonic. Nobody at the table is examining your drink. They just see a glass.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Or just own it without making it about addiction. You don't have to say "I'm in recovery." You can say "I'm not drinking tonight — early meeting," or "I'm on a health kick," or "I'm driving." If they're colleagues and clients, they're not going to interrogate you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Or evaluate whether the dinner is optional. Like, does Carlos HAVE to be at every single client dinner? Can he sometimes send a deputy? Not every networking opportunity is worth your sobriety.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I also wonder — Carlos, could you tell the waiter privately? Like pull them aside before the meal and say "I'll be drinking sparkling water tonight, please keep it full, but don't announce it." They're used to that.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The soda water thing... that could genuinely work. The lime makes it look like a real drink. And Linda's idea about telling the waiter — I could do that too. It takes the topping-off problem out of play. If the waiter isn't pouring wine in my glass, I can't drink it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Multiple options. And notice — these are all tactical solutions. They address the situation without requiring you to white-knuckle through a craving. Prevention before the craving even starts. Carlos, which of these will you commit to trying next time?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The soda water with lime, and telling the waiter in advance. Those two together should cover me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Write those down. Now — about telling your wife. You mentioned you haven't told her. What's stopping you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Shame. She's been so supportive through all of this. She goes to Al-Anon. She removed all the alcohol from our house. She celebrated my 90 days with me. She'd be... disappointed. Hurt maybe.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And what does secrecy do for your recovery?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Nothing good. It keeps me alone with the shame. And... honestly, it's a pattern. Hiding my drinking was what I did for years. Secret drinking, secret bottles, lying about how much. If I'm hiding a slip from her, I'm right back in that pattern. Even if the amount was different.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a profound insight. The behavior pattern of secrecy is itself a relapse warning sign — even if the substance use was limited. I'd encourage you to tell her this week. Not as a confession or an apology — but as an act of transparency. "This happened. Here's what I learned. Here's what I'm doing differently." Can you do that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Tonight. I'll tell her tonight. Better she hears it from me than... I don't know. I just need to not carry it alone.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the shift from isolation to connection. Well done. Tony, let's hear from you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Difficulty rating: a 3. Good week for me. Twenty-two years in the Marines taught me discipline and that's actually serving me well here. I do my meetings, I do my homework, I follow the plan. But I want to be honest — my challenge isn't acute cravings anymore. It's the chronic low-grade desire that never fully goes away. Every single day, somewhere in the back of my brain, there's a little voice saying "you could have a drink." It's not loud. It's more like a hum. Does that ever go away?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a great question for the group. Who relates to what Tony's describing?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. Absolutely. It's like background noise. I call it "the whisper."

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Same. Even on my best days, there's a part of my brain that knows where every liquor store is on my drive home.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Tony, what you're describing is consistent with research on long-term recovery. For many people, the acute cravings diminish significantly after the first year, but a low-level awareness of alcohol persists. Some people call it "the committee meeting in the back of your head." It's your brain's reward system remembering the shortcut. It doesn't necessarily mean you're going to relapse. It means you stay vigilant. And for many people, that awareness actually becomes protective — it prevents complacency.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So I'm not doing something wrong? I'm eight months sober and still thinking about it every day and I thought that meant I was failing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Not at all. You're normal. The difference between you and someone who relapses isn't the absence of desire — it's the presence of skills and choice. You notice the thought and let it pass. That's recovery.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. That's actually reassuring. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Now, let's shift to our skill-building for today. We're going to role-play high-risk scenarios and practice refusal. I want each of you to think of your most common social situation where you're offered or tempted. We'll pair up and rehearse alternatives. Jason, since you successfully navigated a barbecue this week, would you be willing to play the role of pushy colleague for Carlos? Help him practice the work dinner scenario.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely. Carlos, I'll be your most annoying client. You practice ordering and refusing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Great. Set the scene — you're sitting down at a restaurant, the waiter is coming.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Carlos! Great to see you, man. Let's get this party started. Waiter, can we get a bottle of the Caymus cab? Carlos, you drink red, right?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Actually, I'm good tonight — I'm going to stick with sparkling water. Got an early morning. But please, go ahead and enjoy.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Come on, it's a celebration! We just closed the quarter. One glass isn't going to kill you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I appreciate it, really. I'm just going to pass tonight. [to imaginary waiter] Can I get a Pellegrino with lime, please? In a rocks glass?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: You sure? This is the good stuff. Hundred-dollar bottle.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'm sure. Save more for you guys, right? [laughs] Now tell me about the Q3 projections —

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Perfect. Notice what Carlos did there — he redirected the conversation. He used humor. He made it brief and then pivoted to business. Jason, push one more time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: You know, Carlos, I've noticed you haven't been drinking at these dinners lately. Everything okay?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Everything's great. Just been on a health kick lately. My doctor had me cut back — cholesterol, you know how it is at our age. [laughs] But hey, seriously, those Q3 numbers —

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good redirect. How did that feel, Carlos?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Uncomfortable but manageable. The fourth push was the hardest — when he noticed the pattern. But the cholesterol excuse is something people never question. Everyone over forty has cholesterol problems.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. You don't owe anyone your recovery story. A bland health excuse satisfies most people. Now let's do another pair — Rebecca and Linda. Linda, you be Rebecca's ex-husband on the phone. Rebecca, practice the boundary of moving communication to text.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Rebecca, I need to swap weekends next month. Laura's birthday trip. I need my weekend with the kids moved.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Mark, I hear you, but that's my weekend and I've already made plans with the kids. I'm not able to swap.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: You're being impossible. You only care about controlling things. You always —

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Mark, I'm going to stop you there. I'm not going to continue this conversation by phone. Going forward, I'd like us to communicate about custody scheduling through text or email only. It gives us both time to respond thoughtfully. I'm going to hang up now and you can text me if you have more to discuss.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Beautiful. Clean, boundaried, no escalation. How did that feel?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Terrifying. But also powerful? Like I had the words instead of just rage.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the skill. Prepared responses bypass the emotional hijack. When you've rehearsed, you're operating from prefrontal cortex instead of amygdala. Let's do one more — Marcus and Tony. Tony, you be Marcus's boss giving feedback. Marcus, practice responding without the rage spiral.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Marcus, I need to talk to you about the Henderson report. There were some errors in the data tables. I need you to redo section three.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [takes breath] Okay. Can you point out specifically which tables? I want to make sure I get it right this time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Tables four and six. The numbers don't match the source data. It looked sloppy.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [clenching jaw] Got it. I'll have the corrected version to you by end of day. Anything else?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Marcus, where's your rage right now on a scale of 1-10?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: A 6. When he said "sloppy" I wanted to flip the table. But I breathed. And I reminded myself that he's commenting on the report, not on me as a person. Behavior versus character — like what Kevin and Maria are learning in couples therapy. Wait, wrong group. [laughs] Like what WE talked about — separating the feedback from your identity.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [laughs] Good cross-pollination, Marcus. And you're right — the distinction between "this report has errors" and "you are sloppy/incompetent" is entirely in your interpretation. Your boss said "tables four and six need fixing." Your brain added "and therefore you're worthless." That addition is where the rage comes from.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I do that. I hear any criticism and my brain says "see? They don't respect you." And then I want to feel powerful again. And coke was my power switch.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the cognitive distortion — mind-reading and personalization — is the middle link in your chain between trigger and craving. If we can interrupt THERE, the craving might not even activate. That's work for individual therapy too, but keep noticing it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I will. Thanks.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Excellent work, everyone. Really impressive role-plays today. Now as we close, I want each person to name one high-risk situation they anticipate this coming week and one specific strategy they'll use. Let's go around. Jason?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Family dinner at my parents' Wednesday. There'll be wine on the table. Strategy: bring my own six-pack of fancy root beer so I have something in my hand that feels special, and I'll arrive after the cocktail hour — skip the first-drink ritual entirely.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Solid. You're modifying the environment to suit your needs. Rebecca?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My daughter's school performance is Thursday night — my ex will be there. We'll be in the same room. Strategy: I'm bringing my sponsor Margaret as my plus-one so I have accountability. And I'm parking near the exit so I can leave immediately after without walking past him.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Strategic planning. Exits, accountability, minimized contact. Good. Marcus?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nothing specific planned but payday is Thursday and I always used on payday. That was my routine — cash the check, hit up my guy. The money itself is a trigger. Strategy: I'm giving my debit card to my girlfriend Thursday evening and going straight to a meeting at 7 PM. Cash doesn't go in my pocket.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Removing access to means — same principle as Rebecca removing the vodka. Smart. Linda?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Friday night alone again — kids at their dad's. But this time I have a plan. I'm going to the 7 PM meeting and then Jen and I are getting dessert after. I also signed up to volunteer at the community center Saturday morning, which means I have a reason to go to bed early Friday. No empty evening.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're filling the loneliness void with actual connection. Beautiful. Carlos?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Client dinner Tuesday night. Strategy: tell the waiter privately before anyone arrives — "sparkling water with lime all night, rocks glass." Have my phone under the table and text my wife halfway through for accountability. And if it gets really hard, I have Rebecca's soda water trick and I'll excuse myself to the bathroom to breathe.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Multiple layers. Primary plan, backup plan, emergency plan. Excellent. And you're telling your wife tonight about the slip, which means she'll be your accountability partner Tuesday with full context. Tony?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: VA appointment Monday — those always stress me out. Talking about combat stuff. Last time I left the VA I sat in the parking lot for twenty minutes wanting to go to the bar across the street. Strategy: I scheduled a meeting for 5 PM right after, which means I have somewhere to go. I'm not sitting in that parking lot this time.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good — removing the idle time between trigger and potential use. You're pre-committed to the next step before the hard thing even happens.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Exactly. Idle hands and all that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Excellent planning, everyone. I want to highlight something as we close: every single one of you named your trigger AND your strategy without prompting. Twelve weeks ago, most of you couldn't name your triggers at all. That's growth. That's neural pathways being rewired. Remember — relapse prevention isn't about willpower in the moment. It's about making the plan BEFORE you're in the situation. When you're in the middle of a craving, your prefrontal cortex is offline and your decision-making is compromised. The plan has to already exist. You don't build the fire escape during the fire.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: You build it before.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. You build it right here, together, every week. See everyone next Tuesday. Be safe, reach out if you need to, and remember — your sobriety is worth protecting. Every single day.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [various thank yous and goodbyes]

goals: Relapse prevention skills — managing triggers in social settings
